name: Eiffel Tower
address: Champ de Mars, 5 Av. Anatole France, 75007 Paris
description: The iron lady herself — nothing quite prepares you for the scale of it up close. Climb to the second floor for the best balance of views and crowds.

name: Louvre Museum
description: The world's largest art museum — home to the Mona Lisa, Venus de Milo, and 35,000 other works. Go early or book timed entry to skip the worst queues.

name: Musée d'Orsay
address: 1 Rue de la Légion d'Honneur, 75007 Paris
description: A stunning converted railway station housing the finest collection of Impressionist art anywhere — Monet, Renoir, Van Gogh. Far less overwhelming than the Louvre.

name: Notre-Dame Cathedral
description: Paris's gothic heart, now reopened after years of restoration. The exterior flying buttresses and the rose windows inside are genuinely breathtaking.

name: Sacré-Cœur Basilica
address: 35 Rue du Chevalier de la Barre, 75018 Paris
description: Perched at the top of Montmartre hill, the white domed basilica offers sweeping views across the city. The steep walk up through the artists' quarter is half the fun.

name: Palais Royal Gardens
description: A secret garden hiding in plain sight near the Louvre. The arcaded galleries, striped columns, and fountain make it one of those quiet Paris surprises.

name: Centre Pompidou
description: Europe's largest modern art museum — and architecturally wild, with all the pipes and ducts on the outside. The rooftop view over the Marais is excellent.

name: Marché des Enfants Rouges
description: Paris's oldest covered market (1615) tucked into the Marais. A dozen stalls serve everything from Moroccan couscous to Japanese bento — perfect for a casual lunch standing up.

name: Café de Flore
description: Saint-Germain's legendary literary café where Sartre and Simone de Beauvoir had their morning routines. The croque-monsieur and café au lait are the move.

name: Le Comptoir du Relais
description: Yves Camdeborde's packed Saint-Germain bistro — some of the best value classic French cooking in the city. The charcuterie and roast chicken are extraordinary.

name: Le Jules Verne
description: Alain Ducasse's restaurant on the second floor of the Eiffel Tower. The food is exceptional but honestly the view is the real reason to come. Book months ahead.

name: Versailles Palace & Gardens
description: The Sun King's extraordinary palace, 30 minutes from Paris by RER. The Hall of Mirrors alone is worth it, but budget a full day to explore the formal gardens too.

name: Luxembourg Gardens
description: The Left Bank's beloved park — green chairs around the central fountain, kids sailing model boats, chess players under the chestnut trees. A classic Parisian afternoon.

name: Sainte-Chapelle
description: Thirteen Gothic windows of jewel-colored stained glass covering almost every inch of the upper chapel. Genuinely one of the most beautiful interiors in the world.

name: Père Lachaise Cemetery
description: The world's most visited cemetery — final resting place of Chopin, Oscar Wilde, Jim Morrison, Edith Piaf. More of a sculpture park than a solemn place.

name: Montmartre Art Walk
description: The hilltop village where Picasso, Modigliani, and Toulouse-Lautrec all lived and painted. Place du Tertre still fills with portrait artists; Rue Lepic has the best bakeries.

name: Seine River Cruise
description: An hour and a quarter drifting past the Eiffel Tower, Notre-Dame, and the Louvre from the water. The illuminated evening cruises are especially good.

name: Arc de Triomphe
description: Climb to the rooftop terrace for the best view of the twelve radiating boulevards and straight down the Champs-Élysées to the Louvre. Worth every step.

name: Musée Rodin
description: Rodin's former studio and garden — The Thinker and The Kiss out among roses and boxwood hedges. One of the most peaceful museum visits in the city.

name: Picnic on the Champ de Mars
description: Grab a baguette, cheese, and wine from the nearby Rue Cler market and sprawl on the grass beneath the Eiffel Tower. The most Parisian lunch that exists.

name: Cluny — National Museum of the Middle Ages
description: Built over Roman thermal baths, it houses the extraordinary Lady and the Unicorn tapestry series. Surprisingly unhyped for how good it is.

name: Galeries Lafayette Food Hall
description: The entire basement level is France's best regional products — macarons, cheese counters, charcuterie, fresh pasta. As much a cultural visit as a culinary one.

name: Berthillon Ice Cream
description: Paris's most famous glacier on Île Saint-Louis. The salted caramel and wild strawberry scoops are legendary. Queue is always long; always worth it.

name: Passage des Panoramas
description: Paris's oldest covered arcade (1799) — a labyrinth of stamp dealers, engravers, and excellent bistros under glass ceilings. Few tourists, all atmosphere.

name: Château de Vincennes
description: A medieval royal fortress right on the Paris metro, far less visited than Versailles. The keep is one of the tallest medieval towers in Europe.

name: Belleville Street Art Walk
description: The most concentrated block of street art in Paris — Rue Dénoyez is an ever-changing outdoor gallery. Walk up to Parc de Belleville for skyline views without the tourist crowds.
